text.title: Predictive Analytics in PM — Principles and Applications
text.summary: ML application areas for PM, data quality requirements, and the human-in-the-loop mandate that governs all predictive outputs.

rule.statement: Apply ML only where a PMO decision changes when the model output flips — define the trigger before training.
rule.rationale: A model that produces no decision change has zero value regardless of accuracy. Defining trigger first forces PMO alignment and constrains scope.

rule.statement: Use reference-class forecasting (Flyvbjerg) as the required baseline; beat it before claiming ML adds value.
rule.rationale: Simple comparable-project averages often outperform ML on small portfolios. The baseline prevents theater deployments.

section.title: Application Table
section.p: Target use cases by ML type and PM domain:

list.item: Schedule forecasting: analyze past schedules, predict slip in days (regression); trigger: slip > 14d → escalate to steering.
Budget prediction: detect cost overrun early from EVM + vendor invoice patterns; 15% reduction in overruns documented in PMI AI reports.
Resource optimization: multi-variable allocation across concurrent projects; useful only when portfolio > 20 projects.
Risk detection: pattern recognition in issue velocity, comment cadence, owner-change count; feeds risk register.
Material price forecasting: Bayesian / interval models for volatile commodities (steel, cloud spend, semiconductors).

rule.statement: Require clean, structured historical data with consistent status labels, dated transitions, and numeric actuals before training any model.
rule.rationale: Garbage-in/garbage-out dominates PM datasets. Most trackers have inconsistent labels, undated status changes, and missing actuals — no model survives that.

rule.statement: Predict intervals, not points: express schedule slip as "8 days ± 14 (P80)" rather than "8 days".
rule.rationale: Interval forecasts guide better decisions and communicate uncertainty honestly; point predictions create false precision.

rule.statement: Use GroupKFold cross-validation grouped by project or program — never random splits.
rule.rationale: Random splits leak future project data into past, producing optimistically biased evaluation metrics.

antipattern.reason: Survivorship bias: killed projects are excluded, making the training set systematically optimistic about completion outcomes.

antipattern.description: Shipping a model without a drift monitor.
antipattern.reason: Organisational change, new domains, and new tooling break models trained on prior projects within 6-18 months. Silent failure ensues.

rule.statement: The model produces probability + explanation; a human retains all decision authority — no auto-action on budget or schedule.
rule.rationale: Decision-rights confusion is the top governance failure: agents schedule "auto re-baseline on slip prediction" when PM decisions belong to humans.

rule.statement: Ship SHAP values with every prediction; a black-box risk-register entry will be ignored.
rule.rationale: Explainability is non-optional in PM governance contexts. Stakeholder trust collapses on unexplained model outputs.

list.item: Mandatory human checkpoints: training-data contract approval, model promotion to prod, threshold changes, deprecation, any auto-action policy.
LLMs orchestrate — they draft data contracts, explain SHAP outputs, and propose features. They do not do numeric prediction.
Confabulated metrics: agents invent MAE/R²/AUC without running code; force metrics to come from sklearn output captured by a tool call.
